# Clade Reference: Neuronal Line — v0.3

*Working document. v0.3. Do not cite externally.*

---

## How to read this document

Each entry covers one cell of the ABHOP framework's neuronal line. Cells appear in framework order: Bilaterian, Vertebrate, Mammal, Primate. Within each entry, Venn table labels are pulled verbatim from `Inheritance_Lines_v3_0.md`. Citations are sourced from `extractions/` subfolders of the Smithers repository; traceability is marked HIGH (full APA confirmed in extraction), MODERATE (author/year with partial journal details, or reconstructed from reconnaissance text), or LOW (secondary reference, unverified). Claims inferred rather than directly supported by extractions are flagged [INFERRED].

---

## Cell 1: Bilaterian

**What the cell is.** The Bilaterian cell covers animals with a bilateral body plan and a centralized nervous system — a group that first appears in the Cambrian and includes most living animal phyla. The defining substrate innovation is the monoamine system: dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine circuits that let an animal modulate its own arousal and direct movement toward or away from stimuli. This cell introduces the motivation **Boldness** (Effector slot) into the framework.

**The milestone.** The Steering breakthrough marks the first time nervous tissue could orient an animal's whole body toward a goal or away from a threat — directed locomotion with a front-end bias (cephalization). [INFERRED: the precise Cambrian timing of monoamine-mediated steering is not established from Smithers extractions; timing is approximate.]

**Key references**

| Citation | Traceability | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Mather, J. A., & Anderson, R. C. (1993). Personalities of octopuses (*Octopus rubescens*). *Journal of Comparative Psychology, 107*(3), 336–340. | HIGH | First behavioral-personality documentation in a bilaterian invertebrate |
| Sinn, D. L., Apiolaza, L. A., & Moltschaniwskyj, N. A. (2006). *Journal of Evolutionary Biology, 19*(5), 1437–1447. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1420-9101.2006.01136.x | HIGH | Heritable personality dimensions in cuttlefish |
| Stern, S., Kirst, C., & Bargmann, C. I. (2017). *Cell, 171*(7), 1649–1662.e10. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2017.10.041 | HIGH | Neuromodulatory state switches individual variation in *C. elegans* |
| Liang, Z. S., Nguyen, T., Mattila, H. R., Rodriguez-Zas, S. L., Seeley, T. D., & Robinson, G. E. (2012). *Science, 335*(6073), 1225–1228. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1213962 | HIGH | Catecholamine-pathway enrichment in novelty-seeking honeybee scouts |
| Briffa, M., Rundle, S. D., & Fryer, A. (2008). *Proceedings of the Royal Society B, 275*(1640), 1305–1311. https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2008.0025 | HIGH | Repeatable boldness in hermit crabs across risk contexts |
| Niemelä, P. T., Lattenkamp, E. Z., & Dingemanse, N. J. (2015). *Behavioral Ecology, 26*(3), 936–946. https://doi.org/10.1093/beheco/arv036 | HIGH | Boldness–activity behavioral syndrome in field crickets |

**Fun specifics**

- Octopuses show active defense, passive defense, and alertness responses that cluster consistently within individuals — behavioral personality documented in an animal whose nervous system is largely distributed through its arms (Mather & Anderson, 1993).
- *C. elegans* has only 302 neurons, yet neuromodulatory state (serotonin, dopamine, neuropeptides) produces individual-level foraging variation that persists across hours, demonstrating that personality-level variation does not require a large brain (Stern et al., 2017).
- Honeybee scouts that recruit nestmates for new nest sites show gene-expression profiles enriched for catecholamine pathways — suggesting that monoamine modulation of exploration drive was in place before vertebrate nervous systems appeared (Liang et al., 2012).
- Hermit crabs consistently differ in how quickly they re-emerge from their shell after a simulated predator strike, a repeatable boldness measure in an animal with a distributed nervous system and no cortex (Briffa et al., 2008).
- Field crickets show a boldness–activity correlation across risk contexts, a behavioral syndrome linking Foraging Style to Affective Style at the Bilaterian level — the earliest clade at which cross-context trait correlations have been formally measured (Niemelä et al., 2015).
- The four classical conditioning heuristics — eligibility trace, overshadowing, latent inhibition, and blocking — are documented in invertebrates, but no Smithers extraction covers their neurochemical substrate in bilaterians. [INFERRED from secondary literature; not sourced from extractions.]

**Smithers coverage.** The `Coping Style Among Invertebrate Bilaterians` field reconnaissance is the primary source for this cell and provides dense empirical coverage of behavioral personality and repeatability across bilaterian invertebrate taxa. Coverage of monoamine substrate origins, conditioning heuristic mechanisms, cephalization, and Cambrian behavioral radiation is absent from all Smithers extractions; those claims are marked INFERRED or omitted. **Density: MODERATE** (rich for behavioral syndrome evidence, thin for neurological founding mechanisms).

---

## Cell 2: Vertebrate

**What the cell is.** The Vertebrate cell covers jawed vertebrates — fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals before mammalian-specific innovations are added. The defining substrate innovations are the basal ganglia's actor-critic circuitry and the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, which together enable reinforcement learning and calibrated stress physiology. This cell introduces the motivation **Mastery** (Regulator slot) and places the **Behavioral Syndrome** in the Operator position — the recognition that behavioral traits co-vary across contexts.

**The milestone.** The Reinforcing breakthrough marks the first nervous system that could assign temporal credit — learning which of many prior actions caused a reward or punishment that arrived seconds or minutes later. [INFERRED: the claim that temporal credit assignment first emerges with vertebrate basal ganglia is not sourced from Smithers extractions; extractions cover behavioral syndrome correlates, not the basal ganglia substrate directly.]

**Key references**

| Citation | Traceability | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Koolhaas, J. M., Korte, S. M., De Boer, S. F., Van Der Vegt, B. J., Van Reenen, C. G., Hopster, H., De Jong, I. C., Ruis, M. A. W., & Blokhuis, H. J. (1999). Coping styles in animals: Current status in behavior and stress physiology. *Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews, 23*, 925–935. | HIGH | Defines proactive/reactive coping dichotomy and SNS–HPA dissociation across vertebrates |
| Koolhaas, J. M., de Boer, S. F., Buwalda, B., & van Reenen, K. (2007). Individual variation in coping with stress. *Brain, Behavior and Evolution, 70*, 218–226. | HIGH | Cross-vertebrate coping style variation; dopamine coupling in proactive copers |
| Koolhaas, J. M., de Boer, S. F., Coppens, C. M., & Buwalda, B. (2010). Neuroendocrinology of coping styles. *Frontiers in Neuroendocrinology, 31*, 307–321. | HIGH | Neuroendocrine substrate of coping style; HPA–SNS dissociation mechanism |
| Bell, A. M., Hankison, S. J., & Laskowski, K. L. (2009). The repeatability of behaviour: A meta-analysis. *Animal Behaviour, 77*, 771–783. | HIGH | Mean repeatability R = 0.37 across taxa; methodological anchor for behavioral syndrome research |
| Sih, A., Bell, A. M., & Johnson, J. C. (2004). Behavioral syndromes: An ecological and evolutionary overview. *Trends in Ecology & Evolution, 19*(7), 372–378. | MODERATE | Founding framework paper for behavioral syndromes; APA reconstructed, not verbatim in extractions |
| Royauté, R., Berdal, M. A., Dochtermann, N. A., & Dingemanse, N. J. (2018). Paceless life? A meta-analysis of the pace-of-life syndrome hypothesis. *Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology, 72*, 64. | HIGH | Behavior–physiology correlations in POLS largely unsupported at individual level after correction |

**Fun specifics**

- Proactive copers hold territory aggressively, habituate quickly to novel stimuli, and show SNS-dominant stress profiles; reactive copers freeze, remain hypervigilant, and show HPA-dominant profiles — a reliable two-tier dissociation replicated across fish, rodents, pigs, and primates (Koolhaas et al., 1999).
- The basal ganglia's dopaminergic reward-prediction error signal — firing above baseline when reward exceeds expectation, below baseline when expectation exceeds reward — is the neural implementation of the Assign Temporal Credit capacity. [INFERRED: basal ganglia mechanism not sourced from Smithers extractions.]
- Behavioral syndrome correlations average r ≈ 0.20 after publication-bias correction, and mean behavioral repeatability is R = 0.37 — weak but consistent enough to sustain natural selection on correlated trait suites (Bell et al., 2009; Royauté et al., 2018).
- Pace-of-life syndrome predicts that bold, exploratory individuals should also show elevated resting metabolism and faster development — but individual-level behavior–physiology correlations are largely unsupported in current meta-analytic data (Royauté et al., 2018).
- The enteric nervous system — roughly 500 million neurons lining the gut — provides a plausible substrate for the Gut Sense capacity; it operates with substantial autonomy from the brain and generates signals that influence decision-relevant states without reaching cortex. [INFERRED: enteric system as Gut Sense substrate not verified from Smithers extractions.]
- Dopamine coupling means that proactive copers show stronger dopaminergic responses to reward cues — a mechanism linking Coping Style to the Reinforcing breakthrough at the neurochemical level (Koolhaas et al., 2007).

**Smithers coverage.** The `Behavioral syndromes methodology` extraction provides good statistical grounding for the Behavioral Syndrome position but minimal neuroscience. The three Koolhaas coping papers are sourced from the mammal-targeted `Affiliation Architecture` extraction rather than a Vertebrate-specific source. Basal ganglia actor-critic substrate is absent from all Smithers extractions. **Density: MODERATE** (Coping Style well-sourced, Reinforcing mechanism and basal ganglia substrate largely inferred).

---

## Cell 3: Mammal

**What the cell is.** The Mammal cell covers placental and marsupial mammals. The defining substrate innovations are the agranular prefrontal cortex and the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, which together enable sustained affiliation, generative simulation, and allostatic regulation of social behavior. This cell introduces the motivation **Affiliation** (Modeler slot) and places the **Self-Model** in the Connector position.

**The milestone.** The Simulating breakthrough marks the first nervous system that could run internal forward models — generating predictions about future states rather than only evaluating past states. This is the neurological basis for mammalian play, REM sleep consolidation, and rehearsal of behavior before execution. [INFERRED: the specific link between agranular PFC and generative forward simulation follows Friston's active inference framework, which is absent from all Smithers extractions.]

**Key references**

| Citation | Traceability | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Koolhaas, J. M., Korte, S. M., De Boer, S. F., Van Der Vegt, B. J., Van Reenen, C. G., Hopster, H., De Jong, I. C., Ruis, M. A. W., & Blokhuis, H. J. (1999). Coping styles in animals: Current status in behavior and stress physiology. *Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews, 23*, 925–935. | HIGH | Coping Style substrate continuity from Vertebrate cell; mammalian elaboration |
| Feldman, R. (2017). The neurobiology of human attachments. *Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 21*, 80–99. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2016.11.007 | HIGH | OXT-ANS biobehavioral synchrony in caregiver–infant dyads; attachment as Mammal-level mechanism |
| Feldman, R., & Eidelman, A. I. (2007). *Developmental Psychobiology, 49*, 290–302. https://doi.org/10.1002/dev.20220 | HIGH | Maternal synchrony predicts infant vagal tone development |
| Kenkel, W. M., Paredes, J., Yee, J. R., Pournajafi-Nazarloo, H., Bales, K. L., & Carter, C. S. (2013). *PLOS ONE, 8*(8), e69965. | HIGH | OXT mediates pair bond formation in prairie voles; HPG–reward circuit intersection |
| Topál, J., Miklósi, Á., Csányi, V., & Dóka, A. (1998). *Journal of Comparative Psychology, 112*, 219–229. https://doi.org/10.1037/0735-7036.112.3.219 | HIGH | Dogs pass Strange Situation test; attachment behavioral system extends to cross-species bonds |
| Gácsi, M., Maros, K., Sernkvist, S., Faragó, T., & Miklósi, Á. (2013). *PLOS ONE, 8*(3), e58475. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0058475 | HIGH | Dogs show proximity seeking and secure-base effects analogous to infant attachment |
| Gettler, L. T., McDade, T. W., Feranil, A. B., & Kuzawa, C. W. (2011). *PNAS, 108*, 16194–16199. | HIGH | Paternal caregiving reduces testosterone within 30 minutes; HPG plasticity in mammalian affiliation |

**Fun specifics**

- Prairie voles form lifelong pair bonds mediated by oxytocin (OXT) and vasopressin (AVP) release in the nucleus accumbens during mating — the same reward circuitry that processes food and drug reinforcement (Kenkel et al., 2013).
- Dogs show the full Strange Situation pattern when separated from their owner: distress on departure, relief on return, use of the owner as a secure base for exploring a novel environment (Topál et al., 1998; Gácsi et al., 2013).
- Maternal synchrony — moment-by-moment matching of an infant's breathing and movement by a caregiver — predicts infant vagal tone at 3 months, suggesting that Autonomic Regulation develops partly through social entrainment (Feldman & Eidelman, 2007).
- Filipino fathers who carry their infants show testosterone drops measurable within 30 minutes — a rapid HPG-axis response to affiliative behavior, consistent with the Simulating cell's HPG innovation (Gettler et al., 2011).
- REM sleep in mammals is the clearest behavioral signature of generative simulation: the motor system is paralyzed but the brain runs through action sequences, and blocking REM impairs procedural memory consolidation. [INFERRED: REM as Simulating mechanism; not sourced from Smithers extractions.]
- Friston's active inference framework — perception and action as joint minimization of prediction error against a generative model — is the most developed formal account of the Simulating breakthrough mechanism. It is absent from all Smithers extractions and cannot be cited here beyond this note. [INFERRED: no extraction source available.]

**Smithers coverage.** The `Affiliation Architecture in Mammals` extraction is the primary source and provides rich coverage of OXT/AVP physiology, caregiver–infant synchrony, and cross-species attachment; the document's own conclusion is that the PNS × HPG architecture is "biologically credible but evidentially unbuilt." Friston active inference, predictive coding, and mammalian play are entirely absent from Smithers extractions. **Density: MODERATE** (attachment substrate dense, Simulating mechanism gap).

---

## Cell 4: Primate

**What the cell is.** The Primate cell covers Old World monkeys, apes, and humans at the clade level. The defining substrate innovations are the granular prefrontal cortex (dorsolateral PFC) and the expanded temporoparietal junction, which together enable modeling other minds as having distinct intentions, beliefs, and goals. This cell introduces the motivation **Autonomy** (Reviser slot) and places the full **Personality** structure in the Operator position.

**The milestone.** The Mentalizing breakthrough marks the first nervous system that could hold a model of another agent's model — theory of mind in its strong form, including the capacity to represent false beliefs. This enables social strategies that depend on tracking others' ignorance as well as their knowledge, and it grounds the capacities for Tactical Deceit and Common Knowledge that appear in this cell's Venn.

**Key references**

| Citation | Traceability | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| King, J. E., & Figueredo, A. J. (1997). *Journal of Research in Personality, 31*, 257–271. | HIGH | First chimpanzee personality factor structure; six factors including a separate Dominance |
| Weiss, A., Staes, N., Pereboom, J. J. M., Inoue-Murayama, M., Stevens, J. M. G., & Eens, M. (2015). *Psychological Science, 26*(9), 1430–1439. | HIGH | Bonobo personality factor structure; Conscientiousness absent, Dominance present |
| Staes, N., Weiss, A., Helsen, P., Korody, M., Eens, M., & Stevens, J. M. G. (2016). *Scientific Reports, 6*, 38193. | HIGH | Bonobo social tolerance linked to personality; sex differences in factor structure |
| Hopkins, W. D., Donaldson, Z. R., & Young, L. J. (2012). *Genes, Brain and Behavior, 11*, 552–558. | HIGH | AVPR1A microsatellite length predicts affiliation and social bond strength in chimpanzees |
| Stimpson, C. D., Barger, N., Taglialatela, J. P., Gendron-Fitzpatrick, A., Hof, P. R., Hopkins, W. D., & Sherwood, C. C. (2016). *Social Cognitive and Affective Neuroscience, 11*(3), 413–422. | HIGH | Spindle neurons in chimpanzee and bonobo anterior cingulate; substrate for rapid social signaling |
| Rilling, J. K., Scholz, J., Preuss, T. M., Glasser, M. F., Errangi, B. K., & Behrens, T. E. (2012). *Social Cognitive and Affective Neuroscience, 7*(4), 369–379. | HIGH | Arcuate fasciculus expansion in humans relative to chimpanzees; social cognition white matter substrate |
| Mouginot, M., Wilson, M. L., Desai, N., & Surbeck, M. (2024). *Current Biology, 34*, 1780–1785. | HIGH | Bonobo female coalitions constrain male aggression; revises uniform low-aggression model |

**Fun specifics**

- Dominance appears as a separate personality factor in all studied non-human primates but is absorbed into Extraversion in human factor structures — a taxonomic anomaly that has no agreed explanation and that places a Primate-specific personality dimension below the human species boundary (King & Figueredo, 1997; Weiss et al., 2015).
- Chimpanzee AVPR1A microsatellite length predicts individual variation in affiliation and social bond strength — the same gene linked to pair bonding in prairie voles, operating at the Primate level in a species that does not form pair bonds (Hopkins et al., 2012).
- Bonobo females form coalitions that collectively suppress male aggression, a finding that revises the earlier model of bonobo society as uniformly low-aggression and positions female Alliances as the primary social organizing mechanism in one of humanity's two closest relatives (Mouginot et al., 2024).
- Spindle neurons (von Economo neurons) appear in chimpanzee and bonobo anterior cingulate cortex — a cell type previously believed to be human-specific and associated with rapid integration of social and emotional signals (Stimpson et al., 2016).
- The strong-form social brain hypothesis — that primate neocortex size scales with social group size — has been contested by Bayesian reanalyses showing 95% CIs for predicted group size spanning approximately 2 to 520; diet predicts brain size as well as sociality does (DeCasien et al., 2017, *Nature Ecology & Evolution, 1*, 0112 — MODERATE, APA reconstructed from Innovations reconnaissance extraction, not verbatim).
- False-belief tasks — requiring a subject to represent another agent's incorrect belief rather than reality — are the purest behavioral signature of the Mentalizing breakthrough and are passed by great apes under some conditions, though performance under standard task conditions remains contested. [INFERRED: false-belief performance in non-human apes is contested in current literature; not sourced from Smithers extractions.]

**Smithers coverage.** The `Personality, temperament, and social cognition across Pan` field map is the richest single extraction for any neuronal-line cell — dense annotated source list with full APA, genetics, neuroanatomy, social cognition, and individual differences across both chimpanzees and bonobos. The `V2: Personality factor structure across animal taxa` extraction adds cross-taxon factor structure context. Social brain hypothesis contestation is documented in the `Innovations` reconnaissance. **Density: HIGH**.

---

## Cross-cutting notes

These notes use the three-line stack framing. The genetic line carries genome-encoded trait architecture; the neuronal line carries the nervous system and its accumulated breakthroughs; the symbolic line carries culturally transmitted information. The four cells in this document cover the neuronal line only. Genetic-line and symbolic-line parallels are not developed here.

**Breakthrough progression**

| Cell | Breakthrough | Condensed mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Bilaterian | Steering | Monoamine modulation of directed locomotion |
| Vertebrate | Reinforcing | Basal ganglia dopamine reward-prediction error [INFERRED] |
| Mammal | Simulating | Agranular PFC generative forward model [INFERRED] |
| Primate | Mentalizing | Granular PFC + TPJ model of other minds |

Each breakthrough conserves the prior and adds one operation. Steering → Reinforcing → Simulating → Mentalizing is a cumulative stack, not a replacement series.

**Neuronal line substrate progression**

| Cell | Substrate innovation | ABHOP Venn position |
|---|---|---|
| Bilaterian | Monoamine system | Arousal Type (Adapter) |
| Vertebrate | Basal ganglia / HPA axis | Assign Temporal Credit (Reactor) |
| Mammal | Agranular PFC / HPG axis | Reflective Cognition (Planner) |
| Primate | Granular PFC / TPJ | Common Knowledge (Planner) |

[INFERRED: substrate-to-position mapping is a framework interpretation; not directly sourced from Smithers extractions.]

**Trait structure progression**

Behavioral personality appears at the Bilaterian level as consistent individual differences in single traits. At the Vertebrate level, traits correlate across contexts (the Behavioral Syndrome). At the Mammal level, a Self-Model organizes traits around a coherent individual. At the Primate level, the full five-to-six-factor Personality structure appears, with Dominance as a primate-specific factor absent from human self-report data. The Conscientiousness factor appears in chimpanzees but is absent or reduced in bonobos — a within-primate variation whose cause is unresolved.

**Implicit-model and generative-model regulation**

The Vertebrate cell's Reinforcing breakthrough establishes what machine-learning terminology calls model-free RL. The more accurate label is **implicit-model RL**: the environmental representation is not absent but compressed into policy parameters. Both implicit-model and generative-model (model-based) RL satisfy the Good Regulator theorem, which requires only a homomorphic image of the environment somewhere in the regulator; in implicit-model RL that image is the policy parameters themselves, not a separable model object.

Stanovich's tripartite model of cognition maps directly onto the R-slot sequence:

| Stanovich level | Processing type | ABHOP slot |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous mind (Type 1) | Fast, automatic, parallel; pattern-matching and reflex | R1 Effector |
| Algorithmic mind (lower Type 2) | Computationally costly, decoupled, recipe-following | R2 Regulator |
| Reflective mind (upper Type 2) | Goal-setting, monitoring, episodic simulation | R3 Modeler |

The Vertebrate constellation (R1+R2+R4, no foreground R3) is structurally implicit-model regulation. R3 enters at the Mammal cell. This explains why even fully configured humans spend most behavioral time running R2 machinery: R2 is metabolically cheap; R3 deliberation is recruited only when stored procedures fail or novel constellations demand explicit forward planning.

Niche-construction (Odling-Smee, Laland, Feldman) is implicit-model regulation at the lineage level. A beaver building a dam executes a stored procedure — R2 algorithmic machinery — with no cognitive deliberation required. The environmental model exists at the lineage level, distributed in gene-regulatory architecture as developmental memory, and is implemented at the individual level as a recipe the organism runs.

The Mammal cell's Simulating breakthrough installs **generative-model regulation** — structurally active inference. A generative model is a joint probability distribution over observations and hidden states, with separable components (likelihood mapping, transition dynamics, preferences) that can be updated independently of the action policy. Four capabilities follow that implicit-model regulators cannot deliver:

*Sensory-array prediction error.* Implicit-model RL predicts one scalar reward. Active inference predicts the full sensory array; the information-per-trial is orders of magnitude higher, and the error signal can drive update at any level of a cortical hierarchy.

*Epistemic value as a formal drive.* Expected free energy decomposes into pragmatic value (preferences over observations) and epistemic value (expected uncertainty reduction about hidden states). Curiosity, novelty-seeking, and exploration in the absence of reward fall out of the math as a separate functional gradient — not an ad hoc value-function addition.

*Off-line counterfactual simulation.* The generative model runs forward over imagined action sequences without acting. Hippocampal replay during sleep and quiet waking is this in operation: the model used during behavior re-runs on cached experience to update parameters off-line.

*Unified perception-action.* The same generative model that predicts sensory consequences of motor commands does perceptual inference. Predictive coding in sensory cortex and active inference in motor cortex are two routes through one machinery.

Empirical anchors: Tolman's latent learning (rats acquired cognitive maps without reward delivery); place cells and grid cells in hippocampus and entorhinal cortex (spatial component of the generative model); hippocampal replay during sleep cycles (off-line simulation, well-documented in rodents); novelty preference in habituation studies (epistemic value preceding reward signal).

The framework's claim is architecture-level. The cortex-plus-hippocampus architecture is what makes a separable generative model implementable at the Mammal cell. Whether active-inference mathematics can re-describe earlier learning forms after the fact is a separate question. The architecture is what changed at this cell.

---

## Intellectual Kinships

This section records thinkers, traditions, and bodies of work that the framework absorbs or converges with at the neuronal line. Two patterns organize the entries. **Absorption** means the thinker's vocabulary enters the Venns directly as the canonical content for a specific region. **Convergence** means the thinker arrives at a compatible structural commitment by an independent route without shared vocabulary.

### Absorptions

**Friston.** Karl Friston. Free Energy Principle and active inference framework (2005 onward). Perception and action as joint minimization of prediction error against a generative model. ABHOP absorbs active inference at the Mammal center, the breakthrough Simulating. Active inference and Bennett's Simulating breakthrough are the same construct at the four-way intersection. The Conservator triple at Mammal and at Primate holds Autonomous Cognition. Safron & DeYoung (2021) extends the FEP framework with Big-Five-metatrait grounding, and ABHOP inherits the empirical and theoretical apparatus.

**Bennett.** Max Bennett. *A Brief History of Intelligence: Evolution, AI, and the Five Breakthroughs That Made Our Brains* (2023). Five evolutionary cognitive breakthroughs. Four are the neuronal-line breakthroughs at the framework's centers: Steering (Bilaterians), Reinforcing (Vertebrates), Simulating (Mammals), Mentalizing (Primates). The fifth, Speaking, is the breakthrough at the Band-Human cell. ABHOP absorbs Bennett's vocabulary as the canonical center labels across the neuronal-to-symbolic transition. Bennett also anchors Self-Model, the signature at the Mammal Connector — the mammalian self-representation that pairs with Simulating.

**Bowlby & Ainsworth.** John Bowlby. Attachment trilogy (*Attachment*, 1969; *Separation*, 1973; *Loss*, 1980). Mary Ainsworth. Strange Situation experimental paradigm (1969 onward). The attachment behavior system as the mammalian architecture for maintaining proximity to caregivers, with individual differences sorting into secure, anxious, avoidant, and disorganized patterns. ABHOP places the attachment-style construct at the Mammal, Primate, and Band-Human Anticipator (R2×R3) under Experiencing Style. At the Mammal and Primate Connector (R1×R3) the signature is Self-Model; attachment-style content gives its relational-component reading — the modeling of specific others as agents — rather than the primary anchor of that signature.

**Miczek.** Klaus Miczek. Social defeat paradigm in behavioral pharmacology and neuroscience (1980s onward). Repeated social subordination as a stressor producing lasting behavioral and neuroendocrine consequences. ABHOP treats Social Defeat as an experimental method for studying Koolhaas coping-style architecture — the construct the framework absorbs as Behavioral Syndrome at the Vertebrate R2×R4 pair.

**Deci & Ryan.** Edward Deci and Richard Ryan. Self-Determination Theory (1985 onward). Three basic psychological needs: autonomy, competence, relatedness. ABHOP reads the autonomy-competence-relatedness triad as one anchor of the Agreeableness construct at the R3×R4 Adapter, which debuts at Primate and persists at Band-Human and Settlement-Human. The framework treats self-determination as one anchor of Agreeableness in its mentalization-enabled-autonomous-cooperation form.

**Koolhaas.** Jaap Koolhaas. Two-tier coping-style architecture (1999 onward). Proactive versus reactive coping styles sorting on the SNS-HPA ratio with dopamine as the broadcast neuromodulator. ABHOP absorbs Koolhaas's mechanism as the Behavioral Syndrome construct at the Vertebrate R2×R4 Operator pair, and places Coping Style at the Vertebrate, Mammal, and Primate Controller (R1×R2).

**Sih, Bell & Johnson.** Andrew Sih, Alison Bell, J. Chadwick Johnson. *Behavioral Syndromes: An Ecological and Evolutionary Overview* (TREE, 2004). Cross-lineage empirical anchor: suites of correlated behaviors across contexts documented across fish, birds, and mammals. ABHOP places Behavioral Syndrome at the Vertebrate R2×R4 Operator as the founding form, coupling Mastery (R2) with Excitability (R4), with Koolhaas's architecture supplying the mechanism.

**Barton & Dunbar.** Robert Barton and Robin Dunbar. Social Brain Hypothesis (1990s onward). Primate brain expansion driven by social-complexity selection pressures rather than foraging or tool use. Companion work on tactical deception by Richard Byrne and Andrew Whiten. ABHOP absorbs the Social Brain account at the Primate cell. The tactical-deception work anchors Tactical Deceit at the Pursuer (absent R2). Alliances sits at the Reactor (absent R3). Troop-size selection pressure is the engine of mentalization.

**Colin DeYoung.** Big Five Aspect Scales (BFAS) and Cybernetic Big Five Theory (CB5T). DeYoung's metatraits — Stability and Plasticity — sit atop the Big Five hierarchy, with aspect-level structure (10 aspects) one step down. Safron & DeYoung (2021) extends CB5T with free-energy-principle grounding. ABHOP absorbs BFAS-as-scales at the Empire-Human Venn, with Stability at R1 (Reciprocity) and Plasticity at R3 (Representation). These are single-primary positions whose adjacency to pair regions explains why confirmatory factor analysis recovers them as metatraits.

**HEXACO (Ashton & Lee).** Michael Ashton and Kibeom Lee. HEXACO six-factor personality model (2000 onward). Six factors: Honesty-Humility, Emotionality, eXtraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Openness. ABHOP absorbs HEXACO by distributing the factors across cells rather than preserving them as a unified set. The trait structure that HEXACO's H factor measures lives in the Integrity construct at the Empire-Human Adapter (R3×R4). The Big Five factors map across the symbolic line per the Big Five accretion account in `Inheritance_Lines_v3_0.md`.

**Dark Triad (Paulhus & Williams).** Delroy Paulhus and Kevin Williams. *The Dark Triad of personality* (Journal of Research in Personality, 2002). Three socially aversive factors: Machiavellianism, Narcissism, Psychopathy. ABHOP places the Dark Triad at the Empire-Human Personality Disorders panel: Psychopathy and Narcissism share the Pursuer cell (R1+R3+R4, no R2); Machiavellianism sits at the Planner cell (R2+R3+R4, no R1).

**HiTOP (Kotov et al.).** Roman Kotov and colleagues. Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (2017 onward). Empirically derived dimensional taxonomy of mental disorders organized hierarchically from broad spectra to specific symptoms. ABHOP absorbs HiTOP cluster labels at the Empire-Human Personality Disorders panel. The center holds Pragmatics, Narrative, and Discourse — the three modes of symbolic-line participation whose failure constitutes personality disorder.

**Core Self-Evaluations (Judge et al.).** Timothy Judge, Charlice Hurst, Edwin Locke, Amir Erez. Core Self-Evaluations (CSE) as a higher-order factor encompassing self-esteem, generalized self-efficacy, internal locus of control, and emotional stability. ABHOP places CSE as the empirical anchor of the Self-Evaluation construct at the City-Human R1×R3 pair (Connector axis), persisting at Empire-Human.

**Self-Conscious Emotions (Tracy & Robins; Lewis; Tangney).** Jessica Tracy and Richard Robins; Michael Lewis; June Tangney. Shame, guilt, embarrassment, and pride as evaluative emotions with characteristic appraisals of self against internalized standards. Universal across cultures, emerging developmentally around age three, absent or proto-form in non-human primates. ABHOP places Self-Conscious Emotions as one empirical anchor of the Self-Reference construct at the Band-Human R1×R3 pair (Connector axis), alongside Hofstadter's strange-loop pattern.

### Convergences

**Réale.** Denis Réale. Pace-of-life syndrome framework. Five behavioral dimensions: Boldness, Exploration, Activity, Aggressiveness, Sociability. Réale converges with Koolhaas. The framework reads Réale's syndrome as entailed by the Behavioral Syndrome construct at the Vertebrate R2×R4 Operator. Koolhaas's coping-style mechanism — the proactive-reactive axis on the SNS-HPA ratio — produces correlated variation across four of Réale's five dimensions: Boldness, Exploration, Activity, Aggressiveness. Those four are absorbed at the Vertebrate Operator under Behavioral Syndrome. The fifth dimension, Sociability, is affiliative; it is sourced from the Affiliation motivation entering at Mammal and expressed at Mammal, Primate, and Band-Human as Experiencing Style. This is why the framework treats Réale as convergence rather than as a separate absorption.

**Freud.** Sigmund Freud. Structural model (ego, id, superego). Freud's three-part decomposition of the psyche converges on ABHOP's three-function decomposition at the neuronal line: ego as the executive-regulator (R2), id as the body-effector (R1), superego as the internalized-reference structure (R3). Freudian vocabulary does not enter the Venns. The convergence is at the level of intuition about partition rather than mechanism.

**Carl Jung (general).** Carl Jung. Collective unconscious. Jung's framing converges on ABHOP's eclipsed-infrastructure account: older motivations operating below the foreground window, recurring across cultures because they are infrastructural to all human units rather than because they are inherited culturally. Jung's archetype vocabulary converges on the pattern of recurring constellational positions that retroactively become legible. Jung's vocabulary does not enter the Venns. *Note: the MBTI / Psychological Types convergence is recorded separately in `marges_fallacy_arc_v0_2.md`.*

---
